rule.name: CREATE TABLE must include COMMENT
rule.description: Documentation standard. Every table must have a COMMENT.

rule.name: Disallow Cross-Database Dependencies
rule.description: This rule flags any line where the value of the CROSS_DB column is *cross_db_true*, indicating an invalid cross-database reference.

rule.name: Disallow ACCOUNTADMIN usage in SQL scripts
rule.description: Role escalation risk. Scripts should use least-privilege roles.

rule.name: Disallow COPY INTO without ON_ERROR clause
rule.description: COPY INTO must specify ON_ERROR behavior.

rule.name: Disallow CREATE SCHEMA without IF NOT EXISTS or REPLACE
rule.description:
*Description:* This rule ensures that all CREATE SCHEMA statements explicitly include the IF NOT EXISTS clause to avoid errors when the schema already exists. It enforces safe schema creation practices, particularly important in CI/CD pipelines and automated deployment scripts.
*Why This Matters:* Using IF NOT EXISTS with CREATE SCHEMA prevents execution errors and avoids unnecessary failures when the schema is already present in the database. This contributes to idempotent and robust database deployment scripts.

rule.name: Disallow CREATE TABLE without IF NOT EXISTS or REPLACE
rule.description:
*Description:* This rule ensures that all CREATE TABLE statements explicitly include the IF NOT EXISTS clause to avoid errors when the TABLE already exists. It enforces safe TABLE creation practices, particularly important in CI/CD pipelines and automated deployment scripts.
*Why This Matters:* Using IF NOT EXISTS with CREATE TABLE prevents execution errors and avoids unnecessary failures when the TABLE is already present in the database. This contributes to idempotent and robust database deployment scripts.

rule.name: Disallow CREATE statements with hardcoded database and /or schema prefix
Disallow Cross-Schema Dependencies
rule.description: This rule flags any line where the value of the CROSS_SCHEMA column is *cross_schema_true*, indicating an invalid cross-schema reference.

rule.name: Disallow FLOAT/DOUBLE -- prefer NUMBER(p,s)
rule.description: FLOAT has precision issues. Use NUMBER(precision, scale) for deterministic results.

rule.name: Disallow GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES
rule.description: Over-permissioning risk. Always grant specific privileges instead of ALL.

rule.name: Disallow GRANT Statements to PUBLIC
rule.description:
*Rule Description* Granting privileges to the ``PUBLIC`` role in Snowflake can unintentionally expose objects (such as tables, views, or functions) to all users in the account. This practice increases the risk of unauthorized data access and violates the principle of least privilege.
To maintain a secure and auditable permissions model, avoid using ``GRANT ... TO PUBLIC`` statements. Instead, assign privileges to specific roles or role hierarchies that are tightly controlled

rule.name: Disallow ORDER BY in view definitions
rule.description: ORDER BY in views is ignored by consumers and wastes compute.

rule.name: Disallow SELECT * (force explicit column lists)
rule.description: Explicit column lists prevent breakage when schema changes.

rule.name: Disallow VARCHAR without explicit length
rule.description: Unbounded VARCHAR wastes metadata. Always specify explicit length.

rule.name: Disallow dropping objects without IF EXISTS
Disallow hardcoded USE DATABASE, SCHEMA, or ROLE statements
rule.description: Avoid using hardcoded USE DATABASE, USE SCHEMA, or USE ROLE statements in SQL scripts. These can cause environment-specific dependencies and reduce deployment portability. Use parameterized or environment-driven alternatives instead. Lines starting with -- (comments) are ignored.

rule.name: Disallow plaintext passwords in DDL
rule.description: Security risk. Passwords must not be hardcoded in SQL scripts.

rule.name: Disallow usage of TIMESTAMP types other than TIMESTAMP_TZ
rule.description: Only TIMESTAMP_TZ (with or without explicit precision, e.g., TIMESTAMP_TZ(9)) should be used for handling timestamps to ensure time zone consistency across environments. Usage of TIMESTAMP, TIMESTAMP_NTZ, or TIMESTAMP_LTZ is discouraged because they may lead to ambiguity in time zone interpretation.

rule.name: Dynamic Tables must specify TARGET_LAG
rule.description: Dynamic Tables without TARGET_LAG will fail or use uncontrolled defaults.

rule.name: Dynamic Table names must follow DOM+COMP_MAT_DT_ pattern
rule.description: Pattern: {3-char domain}{1-char component}_{RAW|CUR|AGG|GOL}_DT_{name}

rule.name: Enforce "maturity-level" Code at Positions 5–7
rule.description: Enforce Valid "maturity-level" Codes at Positions 5–7 with Underscore at Position 8 (Case-Insensitive) Description: Ensures that positions 5–7 contain one of *RAW*, *CON*, *AGG*, *DAP*, or *DAM*, and position 8 is an underscore _, ignoring case. Helps maintain strict naming conventions for objects. * RAW_ – Raw data layer * REF_ – Reference data * CON_ – Conformed data * AGG_ – Aggregated data * DAP_ – Data Product * DAM_ – Data marts

rule.name: File Format names must follow DOM+COMP_MAT_FF_ pattern
rule.description: Pattern: {3-char domain}{1-char component}_{RAW|CUR|AGG|GOL}_FF_{name}

rule.name: Schema names must follow DOMAIN_MATURITY_ prefix
rule.description: Schema must start with RAW_, CUR_, AGG_, GOL_, or REF_

rule.name: Schema names must end with _vNNN version
rule.description: Schema must end with _v followed by exactly 3 digits.

rule.name: Stage names must follow DOM+COMP_MAT_ST_ pattern
rule.description: Pattern: {3-char domain}{1-char component}_{RAW|CUR|AGG|GOL}_ST_{name}

rule.name: Stored Procedure names must follow DOM+COMP_MAT_SP_ pattern
rule.description: Pattern: {3-char domain}{1-char component}_{RAW|CUR|AGG|GOL}_SP_{name}

rule.name: Table names must follow DOM+COMP_MAT_TB_ pattern
rule.description: Pattern: {3-char domain}{1-char component}_{RAW|CUR|AGG|GOL}_TB_{name}

rule.name: (dynamic) Table names must begin with a 3-character alphanumeric component code followed by an underscore
rule.description:
*Description:* All table names must follow the naming convention of a three-character sub-component code (letters A–Z or digits 0–9), followed by an underscore _, and then a descriptive name. This promotes consistency, discoverability, and easier identification of owning components.
*Examples:* * IOT_SENSOR_DATA * SENSOR_DATA

rule.name: Task names must follow DOM+COMP_MAT_TK_ pattern
rule.description: Pattern: {3-char domain}{1-char component}_{RAW|CUR|AGG|GOL}_TK_{name}

rule.name: View names must follow DOM+COMP_MAT_VW_ pattern
rule.description: Pattern: {3-char domain}{1-char component}_{RAW|CUR|AGG|GOL}_VW_{name}
